title: Lángos
subtitle: Hungarian deep-fried flatbread with garlic and soured cream
excerpt: Yeasted dough stretched thin and fried in oil until puffed and golden, then rubbed with garlic, smeared with soured cream, and piled with grated cheese. The definitive Hungarian street food — crisp at the edges, doughy at the centre.
sourceNotes: Lángos is one of the most recognisable street foods in Hungary, sold at markets, fairs, and lake resorts throughout the summer and at Christmas markets in winter. The name derives from the Hungarian word for flame (láng), referring to the original method of cooking the dough directly in the embers of a bread oven. Modern lángos is deep-fried, not flame-baked. The toppings vary; soured cream and cheese is the standard, but garlic rubbed directly onto the hot surface is not optional.

recipe.freezeNotes: Lángos should be eaten fresh. The dough can be made, shaped, and refrigerated overnight; fry directly from the fridge, adding 1 minute to the frying time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be prepared and left to rise in the refrigerator overnight. Shape and fry to order.

content.text: Lángos dough is a simple yeasted flatbread dough: flour, milk, a little yeast and sugar, salt. It needs one rise of about an hour, then it is stretched by hand (not rolled — rolling presses out the air) to an oval about 5 mm thick and slipped into hot oil. The dough puffs dramatically as it fries, creating a crisp, blistered exterior over a soft interior.

The oil needs to be at 170 to 180°C — hot enough to puff the dough and colour it quickly, but not so hot that the outside burns before the inside cooks through. A large saucepan with at least 5 cm of oil is the right setup. Fry one at a time.

Mix the {{yeast-dried}} and {{granulated-sugar}} into the warm {{whole-milk}} and leave for 5 minutes until frothy. Combine with the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} and mix until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Cover and leave to rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled.

Heat the {{sunflower-oil}} in a large saucepan to 175°C. Divide the dough into four equal pieces. Working with one piece at a time, stretch it by hand on an oiled surface to an oval about 5 mm thick, leaving a thicker rim. Carefully lower into the hot oil — it will puff immediately. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes per side until deep golden. Remove and drain on kitchen paper.

While still hot, rub each lángos all over with a cut {{garlic}} clove. Spread generously with {{soured-cream}}, scatter with {{gruyere}}, and season with {{sea-salt-fine}}. Eat immediately — lángos does not keep.

items.cause: Oil not hot enough, or dough too thick
items.fix: Check the oil temperature before frying — it should register 170 to 180°C. Stretch the dough thinner: 5 mm or less. Thick dough fries through but does not puff

items.symptom: Dough absorbing too much oil and greasy
items.cause: Oil too cool, or dough not drained immediately
items.fix: Fry at a higher temperature and drain on kitchen paper immediately. Cool oil causes the dough to absorb rather than fry

items.symptom: Dough not rising in the hour
items.cause: Yeast not activated, or milk too hot and killed the yeast
items.fix: Warm milk should be body temperature (37°C). If it is hotter than comfortable on your wrist, let it cool before adding the yeast

content.text: Lángos is the Hungarian beach food: sold at Balaton lakeside stalls throughout summer, dripping with soured cream and cheese, eaten with both hands while standing up. It is also sold at Christmas markets across Central Europe (Austria, Czech Republic, Slovakia), where it is often called Langos and served as a winter street snack. At home it is made as a weekend treat, fried to order in a large pot of oil. There is no particularly elegant way to eat it.
